answers.field_label: How Will The Funds Be Used
answers.value:
We are requesting RoundUP Program funds to support the launch and first full growing season of the Stoughton Youth Community Garden Initiative, a small but structured youth-led garden program located on a 1/3-acre parcel behind the Stoughton Public Library.

Specifically, the funds will be used for:

1. Infrastructure and Garden Setup (estimated $1,200–$1,500)
- Lumber and hardware for 8 raised beds: untreated cedar boards, screws, corner brackets, and weed barrier fabric. Each bed will be 4 ft x 8 ft and 18 inches deep, allowing younger children and seniors to work comfortably without bending to ground level.
- Soil and compost: delivery of approximately 10 cubic yards of organic topsoil/compost mix from a local supplier in Dane County, plus several bags of specialized soil amendments (e.g., peat-free seed starting mix, organic fertilizer) for starting seeds.
- Basic irrigation: a hose reel, heavy-duty hoses, adjustable nozzles, and a simple drip irrigation kit to reduce water waste and make watering manageable for youth and volunteers. We intend to connect to an existing outdoor spigot at the library with permission.
- Storage and security: a weatherproof storage deck box or small shed for tools, plus a simple combination lock system so that registered youth and adult volunteers can access tools while still keeping them secure.

2. Tools and Supplies for Youth Participants (estimated $600–$800)
- Hand tools sized for children and teens: trowels, cultivators, small rakes, and kid-sized gloves (multiple sizes) so that children as young as 8 can participate safely and comfortably.
- A limited number of adult-sized tools (shovels, hoes, rakes, a wheelbarrow) for older teens and supervising adults.
- Seeds and starter plants: a variety of vegetable, herb, and pollinator-friendly flower seeds, focusing on high-yield and culturally meaningful foods such as tomatoes, peppers, beans, snap peas, cucumbers, kale, collard greens, cilantro, basil, and sunflowers. We will purchase some organic seedlings from local nurseries to ensure a successful first year while also starting some seeds indoors with the teens.
- Educational materials: laminated plant identification cards, a simple garden journal for each youth participant, clipboards, and printed lesson handouts (covering topics such as composting, pollinators, soil health, and basic cooking with garden produce).

3. Program Support and Accessibility (estimated $400–$500)
- Stipends for teen peer leaders: small, modest stipends for 3–4 high school students who will help plan lessons, lead younger children in garden tasks, and assist with translation for families whose first language is not English (primarily Spanish and Hmong in our group). These stipends will be modest but meaningful, signaling that their leadership and time are valued.
- Accessibility improvements: simple seating (two sturdy benches) so that older adults and caregivers with mobility limitations can still participate and supervise, and a few kneeling pads for participants with knee or back issues.
- Printing bilingual outreach flyers (English/Spanish, and simple Hmong-language invitations) to distribute through the schools, the library, and local churches to ensure that participation is not limited to families already connected to existing programs.

4. Community Harvest and Sharing Events (estimated $200–$300)
- Supplies for 2–3 small community harvest days: paper plates, compostable utensils, napkins, and basic ingredients like olive oil, vinegar, whole-grain pasta, and spices so youth can prepare simple, healthy tasting samples (e.g., garden salsa, veggie wraps, or pasta salad with fresh tomatoes and basil).
- Modest signage: a simple, weather-resistant sign identifying the garden as the “Stoughton Youth Community Garden” and acknowledging support from the RoundUP Program so community members understand what the space is for and who it serves.

We will track all expenses carefully, keep receipts, and share a brief summary report with photos at the end of the season so that RoundUP Program staff can see exactly how the funds were used and the tangible outcomes for local youth and families.

answers.value:
The Stoughton Youth Community Garden Initiative is designed to directly benefit the Stoughton community in several overlapping ways: youth development, food access, community connection, and neighborhood pride.

1. Youth Development and Education
Our primary focus is local youth, particularly middle and high school students who may not otherwise have access to hands-on gardening or nutrition education. Many of the teens we work with through the Stoughton Public Library’s after-school programs and the Stoughton Area School District have expressed interest in learning how to grow their own food but do not have a yard, tools, or a family member with gardening experience.

Through this garden, youth will:
- Learn practical skills: planting, watering, weeding, composting, harvesting, and basic food safety. We will structure weekly sessions from May through September, where youth rotate through tasks and short mini-lessons.
- Develop leadership: older teens will be given specific responsibilities such as planning planting layouts, tracking watering schedules, and mentoring younger participants. For example, one of our student leaders, Sofia (age 16), has already volunteered to coordinate a small herb garden area and lead a mini-workshop on using fresh herbs in simple recipes.
- Build confidence and ownership: youth will see a direct, visible result of their efforts—watching seeds turn into plants and plants into food they can share with their families. For many teens, this kind of tangible accomplishment is rare compared to traditional classroom work.

2. Improved Access to Fresh, Healthy Food
While Stoughton is not a large city, we know from school social workers and the local food pantry that some families struggle with consistent access to fresh produce, especially toward the end of the month. Our garden will:
- Donate a portion of the harvest to the Stoughton Food Pantry on a regular schedule, focusing on vegetables that are in high demand and easy to use (tomatoes, cucumbers, peppers, leafy greens, and herbs).
- Allow participating families to take home produce each week. We will make sure there is no cost to participate and no expectation of prior gardening knowledge. For instance, one of our pilot families, a single mother with two children at Fox Prairie Elementary, has already shared that she would love to be able to bring home fresh greens and tomatoes for salads instead of relying on canned vegetables.
- Provide simple recipes and tastings that help families understand how to use the produce in everyday meals. We will keep recipes budget-conscious and culturally flexible (e.g., beans and tomatoes used in chili, rice bowls, or pasta dishes).

3. Community-Building and Cross-Generational Connections
The garden will be located in a very accessible, central spot behind the Stoughton Public Library, which is already a natural gathering place for children, teens, and older adults.

Benefits to the broader community include:
- A welcoming outdoor space: The garden will provide a small, attractive green space where community members can sit, observe, and occasionally join in. We envision weekly open “drop-in” hours where anyone can help with simple tasks like watering or harvesting.
- Cross-generational learning: We have already spoken informally with a few older residents, including a retired teacher and a long-time local gardener, who are excited to volunteer as “garden grandparents.” They will share their knowledge, help troubleshoot plant problems, and connect with youth who might not otherwise have consistent relationships with older adults.
- Civic pride: By turning an underused patch of land into a productive and beautiful space, we hope to inspire similar small-scale projects in other parts of Stoughton. The garden will be clearly labeled as a community initiative, and we will invite neighbors and passersby to our harvest days.

4. Inclusion and Cultural Connection
Stoughton’s population is gradually becoming more diverse, and we want the garden to reflect and celebrate that.
- We will solicit input from families and youth about what foods matter to them culturally. For example, a Hmong family has already asked if we can try growing certain leafy greens they remember from their home gardens, and one of our Spanish-speaking families requested cilantro, jalapeños, and tomatillos.
- Our materials (flyers and basic signs) will be available in English and Spanish, and we will work with a local volunteer to help us create simple Hmong-language invitations. This will help families feel that the garden truly belongs to them, not just to English-speaking residents.

5. Support for Local Institutions and Programs
The project strengthens and complements existing Stoughton resources:
- The Stoughton Public Library: The garden will enhance the library’s youth programming and summer reading activities. Librarians have already expressed interest in hosting story times and STEM-related activities in the garden space.
- Stoughton Area School District: We are coordinating with a middle school science teacher who has offered to connect interested students with the garden as an informal extension of their science curriculum.
- Local nonprofits and churches: We have begun conversations with a local church that runs a free community meal once a month; they are interested in incorporating our produce when available.

In short, this request will benefit the Stoughton community by giving youth a safe, structured way to learn and lead, supporting families with fresh food and practical skills, and transforming a small piece of land into a shared, welcoming space that reflects our community’s values of care, inclusion, and mutual support.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Background and Motivation:
I, Melissa Hartman, have lived in Stoughton for 11 years and currently work part-time as a youth services assistant at the Stoughton Public Library. Over the past three summers, I’ve helped coordinate small-scale craft and STEM activities, and I’ve noticed that outdoor, hands-on projects consistently draw in kids who do not usually sign up for structured programs. Last year, one of our simple container gardening projects (herbs in pots) was so popular that families kept asking if we would be doing “more gardening stuff” the following summer.

Informal Needs Assessment:
- During our after-school program, I asked a group of about 20 middle school students what kinds of activities they wanted more of. Gardening and cooking came up repeatedly, often from students who do not participate in sports or clubs.
- Two school social workers confirmed that some of their students’ families are stretched thin financially and rely on the food pantry. They expressed support for anything that gives kids a sense of agency and also brings more fresh food into their homes.
- I also spoke with the director of the Stoughton Food Pantry, who said they are always happy to receive locally grown produce, especially tomatoes, peppers, cucumbers, and greens, and that they are willing to help us coordinate regular drop-off times.

Partnerships and Volunteer Support:
- Stoughton Public Library: The library staff, including our director, has given verbal support for using the outdoor space behind the building, pending final approval from the city. They are open to cross-promoting events and integrating garden visits into summer reading.
- Volunteer Master Gardener: A local Dane County Master Gardener, Janet Peterson, has offered to volunteer once or twice a month to help with planning, pest management, and teaching basic gardening skills. She has experience with school gardens and understands how to keep tasks age-appropriate.
- Parent and Community Volunteers: A small group of parents (currently 6 families) has already expressed interest in helping with watering schedules, transportation, and supervising younger children. Some of them work irregular hours but are willing to take on weekend or evening watering shifts.

Inclusivity and Barriers to Participation:
We are committed to making sure that there are no financial, language, or transportation barriers that would prevent youth from participating.
- There is no fee to join, and all supplies will be provided.
- We will schedule most regular sessions immediately after school or early in the evening, so teens can walk or bike from home or school. The library location is central and close to bus routes and common walking paths.
- For families with limited English, we will rely on bilingual teens and a few adult volunteers who have offered to help translate simple instructions and flyers. We also plan to use visuals (pictures of tools, plants, and steps) so younger children and non-readers can follow along.

Sustainability and Long-Term Vision:
While this funding request is focused on the initial setup and first full growing season, we are planning with sustainability in mind.
- Durable infrastructure: The raised beds, tools, and storage purchased this year will be used for multiple growing seasons. We expect to only need to refresh soil and seeds each year, which can be done with smaller fundraising efforts in the future.
- Future funding: After the first year, we plan to apply for small educational and community garden grants, as well as explore sponsorships from local businesses (such as hardware stores, nurseries, or grocery stores) to help cover recurring costs.
- Youth ownership: Over time, we hope to create a small “youth garden committee” where teens help make decisions about what to plant, how to share the harvest, and what kinds of workshops or events to host. The more ownership they feel, the more likely the program will endure beyond any one adult organizer.

Safety and Risk Management:
- All youth participants will have signed permission forms from parents or guardians.
- We will maintain a minimum adult-to-youth ratio of 1:6 during structured sessions.
- Tools will be stored securely when not in use, and we will provide clear safety instructions (e.g., how to carry tools, where to place them when not in use, glove use, and basic sun safety like hats and water breaks).

Why RoundUP Funds Matter:
This initial infusion of support from the RoundUP Program will allow us to move from a good idea that lives mostly in conversations and sketches to a real, functioning garden that Stoughton residents can see, touch, and benefit from this year. Without this kind of start-up funding, we would likely need to delay the project another season while we piece together smaller donations, potentially losing the momentum and enthusiasm we currently have from youth, parents, and community partners.

We are grateful for your consideration and would be happy to share updates, photos, and a brief written summary at the end of the season so you can see the impact of your investment in our community’s young people and families.
